food_subgroup: Braised pork belly in thick coconut cream (kakang gata) with bird's eye chili and bagoong alamang fermented shrimp paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Bicol Express. Closest parent concept: braised pork belly in coconut cream. FoodOn does not currently index Filipino regional dishes at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Bicol Express. Using 'Pork, braised' as the closest parent. The specific preparation with coconut milk and chili is not detailed in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code applicable for this regional Filipino dish preparation.

serving.description: 1 plate (~200g as ulam, served with rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Filipino ulam serving of Bicol Express is approximately 200g (about 6–7 tablespoons of braised pork belly cubes in thick coconut cream sauce), typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) — Bicol Express / braised pork belly in coconut cream; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD for coconut cream fatty acid profile
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table + Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient and micronutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (Philippines) for braised pork belly in coconut cream. Coconut cream saturated fatty acid profile (lauric acid ~44% of coconut fat fraction) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD coconut cream entry. Sodium elevated by bagoong alamang contribution consistent with published Philippine food composition data. Values represent a composite dish estimate at 200g serving size, scaled to per-100g basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for Bicol Express based on analogous low-carbohydrate braised pork dish with coconut cream; reference: University of Sydney GI Database — pork-based dishes and high-fat coconut preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bicol Express in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~32 (low) based on the dish's very low carbohydrate content (4g/100g; 8g per 200g serving) and high saturated fat content from coconut cream, which significantly attenuates glycemic response. GL per 200g serving = (32 × 8g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.56, rounded to 2.6. This is consistent with the GI Database classification of high-fat, low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium (~1,040mg per 200g serving) primarily from bagoong alamang fermented shrimp paste, combined with high saturated fat (35g/serving) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Bicol Express is NOT recommended for hypertensive patients in standard preparation; if consumed, bagoong alamang must be omitted or dramatically reduced (bringing sodium to ~400–500mg/serving), light coconut milk should replace thick cream, and portion size should be limited to 100g or less.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load is very low (~2.6 per 200g serving) due to minimal carbohydrate content, but very high saturated fat (35g/serving) may impair long-term insulin sensitivity via lipotoxicity, and total caloric density (~640 kcal per plate before rice) is a significant concern for weight management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bicol Express has a very low GL and is acceptable for blood glucose control in isolation, but the high SFA content and caloric density require portion restriction to 100–120g and avoidance of pairing with large rice servings to prevent caloric excess and long-term insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extremely high saturated fat content (17.5g/100g; 35g per 200g serving — predominantly lauric acid from coconut cream and palmitic acid from pork belly) combined with high sodium (~1,040mg/serving) and cholesterol (90mg/serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bicol Express poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its saturated fat load exceeding recommended daily limits in a single serving; patients with CVD or dyslipidaemia should avoid regular consumption and, if eating occasionally, limit to a 60–80g portion with bagoong omitted and coconut cream replaced with light coconut milk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (17.5g/100g; 35g/plate) may aggravate systemic inflammation via SFA-activated TLR4/NF-κB signalling pathway; capsaicin from chili provides modest anti-inflammatory benefit via TRPV1 desensitisation but cannot offset SFA pro-inflammatory effect at these quantities
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should avoid regular consumption of Bicol Express; if consuming, reduce pork belly to 60–80g per serving, replace coconut cream with low-fat coconut milk, add anti-inflammatory vegetables (kangkong, bitter gourd), and monitor inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bicol Express is a celebrated Filipino braised pork dish originating from the Bicol region of Luzon, Philippines, and is one of the most iconic examples of Bicolano cuisine. The dish consists of pork belly cubes slow-braised in thick coconut cream (kakang gata) with bird's eye chili (siling labuyo), long green chili (siling haba), and bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste), producing an intensely rich, spicy, and savoury stew. The name 'Bicol Express' refers to the express train that once ran from Manila to the Bicol region, and the dish was popularised under this name at a Manila restaurant before spreading nationwide. Authentic versions are extremely spicy; restaurant and home versions are typically moderated. Per 200g serving (1 standard ulam plate): approximately 640 kcal, 28g protein, 54g fat (35g saturated fat — very high, predominantly lauric acid from coconut cream and palmitic acid from pork belly), 8g carbohydrate, and ~1,040mg sodium (bagoong dominant). Glycemic load is very low (~2.6), making it suitable for blood glucose control in isolation, but the high saturated fat and caloric density are significant concerns for cardiovascular health, hypertension, and long-term insulin sensitivity. NOT Halal due to pork content. Contains shellfish allergen (bagoong alamang). Gluten-free and dairy-free. Commonly served as ulam (main dish) alongside steamed white rice in Filipino households and Filipino restaurants in Singapore (e.g., Lucky Plaza). Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA + IMR Malaysia MyFCD.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) — an approved primary regional source — supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD for coconut cream fatty acid profile. Sodium value is consistent with published bagoong alamang contribution data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology with high confidence (0.9) given the very low carbohydrate content. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated against known nutritional profiles of pork belly and coconut cream components.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data (MUFA/PUFA breakdown) for Bicol Express as a composite dish are not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the dish level; individual component data exist but dish-level composite MUFA/PUFA values are not available in approved sources as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite braised dish is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA at the dish level. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — pork belly and coconut cream contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant sources are absent as pork is not a ruminant). GI estimated at ~32 using analogous-food methodology from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bicol Express exists in the database. Dish is NOT Halal (contains pork). Contains shellfish allergen (bagoong alamang — fermented shrimp paste). The description in the input references ~640 kcal per 200g serving, which is consistent with per-100g energy of 320 kcal × 2. Sodium per 200g serving = 520mg/100g × 2 = 1,040mg, consistent with input description. Saturated fat per 200g serving = 17.5g/100g × 2 = 35g, consistent with input description. All per-serving figures in health_context and llm_training are derived by multiplying per-100g values by 2 (200g serving).
